request.question: Eric Estornel, better known by his stage names Maceo Plex and Maetrik, is an American DJ, producer and DJ Awards winner , he is best known for diverse production style, influenced primarily by house and techno but also tech house, as well as a longtime performer for many Ibiza-based clubs, including Space, was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by who?.

title: Patrick Topping
Steve Lawler
Maceo Plex
Richie Hawtin
Sven Väth
Jamie Jones (DJ)
Space (Ibiza nightclub)
Roger Sanchez
Luciano (DJ)
Tech house
sentences: Patrick Topping (born 25 February 1989) is a British, Newcastle born, DJ and producer.
With releases on Hot Creations, Hottrax and Relief Records Patrick has hit Number 1's on the Beatport Tech House charts, and received DJ Awards for Newcomer DJ and Track Of The Season in Ibiza.

Steve Lawler is a British house music producer and DJ born in Birmingham, England he is a five time DJ Awards winner and has held residences at many popular dance clubs such as Space, The End and Twilo.
Because of his successful performances at Space in Ibiza, he was nicknamed "King of Space".
Lawler has released several mix albums and is especially well known for his "Lights Out" series featured on Boxed.
He currently runs the record label VIVa MUSiC.
He also founded the now defunct Harlem Records.
Eric Estornel, better known by his stage names Maceo Plex and Maetrik, is an American DJ, producer and DJ Awards winner from Dallas and Miami, United States, origin of Cuba.
He now resides in Barcelona, Spain with his wife and kids.
He is best known for diverse production style, influenced primarily by house and techno but also tech house, as well as a longtime performer for many Ibiza-based clubs, including Space and Amnesia.
Richard "Richie" Hawtin (born June 4, 1970) is a Canadian electronic musician and DJ and three-time DJ Awards winner.
He was an influential part of Detroit techno's second wave of artists in the early 1990s and has been a leading exponent of minimal techno since the mid-1990s.
He is best known for his work under the alias Plastikman and for his ENTER.
events in Ibiza and around the world.
Sven Väth (born 26 October 1964) is a German DJ/producer and three time DJ Awards winner whose career in electronic music spans well over 30 years.
His single, “Electrica Salsa” under the alias OFF launched his career in 1986.

Lovingly referred to as “Papa Sven” by his fans, Sven Väth has made his mark in the music community by being one of Germany’s “pop stars” in the nineties, running two famous night clubs in Germany, and starting his own company Cocoon, which encompasses a booking agency, record label, and a branch for events.

He is recognized for cultivating the underground electronic music scene not just in Germany but in Ibiza as well, with his own night at Amnesia for fourteen years and after-parties at creative locations around the island.
Sven Väth is a major proponent of vinyl, using only two decks and a mixer for his extensive DJ sets, his longest set having been 30 hours.
Jamie Jones is a Welsh DJ, producer and two time DJ Awards winner.
He is a member of the electronic music group Hot Natured and the head of the record label Hot Creations, as well as its sub-labels Emerald City and Hottrax.
Jones was voted the top DJ of 2011 by the online dance music magazine Resident Advisor.
In 2012 he received mainstream success with Hot Natured's single "Benediction", which reached number 31 in the UK Singles Chart.
Jones hosts his own "Paradise" party at Ibiza's DC10 nightclub.
In early 2016 Jones launched The House of Hot, an online shop clothing line, vinyls and artwork.
Space ( , ] , ] ) was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by STANCA.
It was awarded "Best Global Club" at the International Dance Music Awards in 2005, 2006, 2012, and again in 2013.
"Space" was located in Playa d'en Bossa on the outskirts of Ibiza Town, close to the airport.
Roger Rene Sanchez (born June 1, 1967) is an American house music DJ, Remixer and Grammy Award Winner for his remix of "Hella Good" by No Doubt in 2003.
He is a four time DJ Awards winner for "Best House DJ" in 1999, 2002, 2004 and 2007 and has received twelve nominations in all.
He won the first International Dance Music Award for Best Podcast in 2007 and has received 8 IDMA nominations for Best American DJ (2003–2010).
Luciano (born Lucien Nicolet on February 24, 1978 in Montcherand, Switzerland) is a Chilean-Swiss DJ and producer he is a five-time DJ Awards winner and ten-time nominee.

He is part of a small group of people who were responsible for developing and nurturing the underground electronic music scene in Chile, via promoting, playing, and throwing parties in the Chilean club circuit.
His DJ sets are strongly influenced by Latin American music, a sound he heavily incorporates.
Over the years, Luciano's initial style of minimal techno has developed and evolved into a blend of house and techno.
Luciano has been constantly involved in various music projects, such as playing live performances, running his label Cadenza Records, and hosting multiple parties in Ibiza.
Tech house is a subgenre of house music that mixes elements of techno with house.

The term "tech house" developed as a shorthand record store name for a category of electronic dance music that combined musical aspects of techno, such as "rugged basslines" and "steely beats," with the harmonies and grooves of progressive house.
The music originally had a clean and minimal production style that was associated with techno from Detroit and the UK.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Eric Estornel, better known by his stage names Maceo Plex and Maetrik, is an American DJ, producer and DJ Awards winner , he is best known for diverse production style, influenced primarily by house and techno but also tech house, as well as a longtime performer for many Ibiza-based clubs, including Space, was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by who?.
hop1_docs: Maceo Plex | Eric Estornel, better known by his stage names Maceo Plex and Maetrik, is an American DJ, producer and DJ Awards winner from Dallas and Miami, United States, origin of Cuba.  He now resides in Barcelona, Spain with his wife and kids.  He is best known for diverse production style, influenced primarily by house and techno but also tech house, as well as a longtime performer for many Ibiza-based clubs, including Space and Amnesia.

Space (Ibiza nightclub) | Space ( , ] , ] ) was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by STANCA.  It was awarded "Best Global Club" at the International Dance Music Awards in 2005, 2006, 2012, and again in 2013.  "Space" was located in Playa d'en Bossa on the outskirts of Ibiza Town, close to the airport.

Steve Lawler | Steve Lawler is a British house music producer and DJ born in Birmingham, England he is a five time DJ Awards winner and has held residences at many popular dance clubs such as Space, The End and Twilo.  Because of his successful performances at Space in Ibiza, he was nicknamed "King of Space".  Lawler has released several mix albums and is especially well known for his "Lights Out" series featured on Boxed.  He currently runs the record label VIVa MUSiC.  He also founded the now defunct Harlem Records.

Luciano (DJ) | Luciano (born Lucien Nicolet on February 24, 1978 in Montcherand, Switzerland) is a Chilean-Swiss DJ and producer he is a five-time DJ Awards winner and ten-time nominee.  He is part of a small group of people who were responsible for developing and nurturing the underground electronic music scene in Chile, via promoting, playing, and throwing parties in the Chilean club circuit.  His DJ sets are strongly influenced by Latin American music, a sound he heavily incorporates.  Over the years, Luciano's initial style of minimal techno has developed and evolved into a blend of house and techno.  Luciano has been constantly involved in various music projects, such as playing live performances, running his label Cadenza Records, and hosting multiple parties in Ibiza.

Switch (house DJ) | David James Andrew Taylor, better known his stage name Switch, is a British DJ, songwriter, sound engineer, and record producer.  He is best known for his work with M.I.A..  In the fidget house genre, Switch runs his own music label "Dubsided", as well as the label Counterfeet, established in 2006 with fellow producer Sinden.  He has released various singles under his own name, and is also well known for remixing and producing for many major artists.  He is a former member of the American electronic dancehall group Major Lazer.

Onur Sasmaz | Onur Sasmaz, commonly known by his stage name DJ Onur or DJ Sasmaz, is a Turkish DJ/producer.  He is best known for the live performing.  Starting out with techno and subsequently moving to hardstyle, they eventually worked in other EDM styles including progressive/electro house.

Thomas Gandey | Thomas Gandey is an English electronic music producer, vocalist, keyboard player and DJ born on 21 March 1976, known for work under his own name as well as under his "Cagedbaby" pseudonym.  He has remixed the likes of Shirley Bassey, Ocean Colour Scene, Empire of the Sun, Fatboy Slim, The Temper Trap and Grace Jones and DJed and performed at many of the world's best-known clubs and festivals, including Womb, Glastonbury Festival, Fuji Rock Festival, and Tokyo's Big Beach Festival.  He has been a resident at We Love Space Ibiza and Barcelona's Razzmatazz.
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage 2 states Space (Ibiza nightclub) "was ... owned by STANCA" (Passage 1 links Maceo Plex to Space). Bridge entity: Space (Ibiza nightclub) and the owner name "STANCA". Missing fact: the owner name is given but the passages do not identify who/what STANCA actually is (no further details). Retrieval directions: 1) Open the "Space (Ibiza nightclub)" page (e.g., Wikipedia) to verify the owner listing and to fetch details explaining who or what "STANCA" refers to (company/individual/legal name).
response.hop2_query: Open the Wikipedia page "Space (Ibiza nightclub)" and extract the owner information. Then follow the page’s references and search reputable sources for "STANCA" + "Space Ibiza" to determine STANCA’s full legal name and whether it is a company or individual (e.g., search terms: "Space Ibiza owner STANCA", "STANCA Space Ibiza who is STANCA", "Space Ibiza sold to STANCA"). Provide citations.
hop2_docs: Space (Ibiza nightclub) | Space ( , ] , ] ) was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by STANCA.  It was awarded "Best Global Club" at the International Dance Music Awards in 2005, 2006, 2012, and again in 2013.  "Space" was located in Playa d'en Bossa on the outskirts of Ibiza Town, close to the airport.

Radu Stanca National Theatre | The Radu Stanca National Theatre (Romanian: "Teatrul Național "Radu Stanca" Sibiu" ) is the largest and the most important establishment of performing arts in Sibiu, Romania.  Founded in 1949 as the "State Theatre of Sibiu", it was given the name of the Romanian playwright and theatre director Radu Stanca.
Doamna Stanca National College | Doamna Stanca National College (Romanian: "Colegiul Naţional "Doamna Stanca"" ) may refer to one of two educational institutions in Romania:

Doamna Stanca National College (Satu Mare) | Doamna Stanca National College (Romanian: "Colegiul Naţional Doamna Stanca") is a public day high school for students aged 10 to 18, established in 1816, and located in Satu Mare, Romania.  The school is named after the spouse of the Romanian ruler Michael the Brave.  The Doamna Stanca National College is one of the only four national colleges located in Satu Mare alongside Mihai Eminescu, Ioan Slavici and Kölcsey Ferenc.
Stanca Act | The Stanca Act is an Italian law of 2004 that promotes the accessibility of information technology.  The law applies also to the Italian government websites.
Răzvan Stanca | Răzvan Marian Stanca (born 18 January 1980, Bucharest) is a Romanian professional football player who plays for Pandurii Târgu Jiu .
Doamna Stanca | Doamna Stanca (died 1603) was the wife of Michael the Brave.  Tradition says that they were married in the Proieni church, Vâlcea County, in 1584.
response.summary_2: Space (Ibiza) was owned by STANCA. The club was located in Playa d'en Bossa on the outskirts of Ibiza Town, and Maceo Plex is noted as a longtime performer there; the provided passages do not explain who or what "STANCA" specifically is.
